data.title: How far is China willing to go to help Cuba in face of increasing US pressure?
data.description:
China is expected to be cautious about confronting the United States over Cuba as Washington ramps up its threats following the abduction of former Venezuelan president Nicolas Maduro.
Since the raid on Caracas, the White House has increasingly turned its attention towards the western hemisphere’s only Communist state, along with Colombia and Greenland, with President Donald Trump saying “Cuba looks like it’s ready to fall”.
Havana is also heavily reliant on subsidised Venezuelan oil and is likely to become increasingly vulnerable if Trump’s administration gains greater control of that supply.
On Thursday, Trump told The New York Times that he expected the US to be running Venezuela and extracting oil from its huge reserves for years.
Any US action against Cuba would not “necessarily be a military operation”, according to Jiang Shixue, director of the Centre for Latin American Studies at Shanghai University.
“However, in other respects, such as through more severe sanctions, the US could still exert pressure on Cuba and once Venezuela cuts off its oil supply, Cuba’s economic situation would deteriorate further,” Jiang said.
Cuba, an island of fewer than 10 million people, is only 145km (90 miles) south of Florida and has been subject to a US economic blockade since the early 1960s.
Its economy barely recovered from the sudden withdrawal of support after the collapse of the Soviet Union, and since then has been grappling with severe shortages, blackouts and the collapse of tourism after the Covid-19 pandemic – all exacerbated by renewed sanctions after Trump returned to the White House.
Trump and his aides have expressed confidence that without oil supplies from Venezuela, Cuba was likely to collapse.
Shortly after the US Army’s Delta Force breached the Venezuelan president’s fortified compound and took away Maduro on Saturday, Trump told reporters that no action would be needed against Cuba.


“I don’t know if they’re going to hold out, but Cuba now has no income,” he added. “They got all their income from Venezuela, from Venezuelan oil.”
Some 32 Cuban military and intelligence personnel who were acting as a security detail for Maduro were killed in the raid.
US Secretary of State Marco Rubio, the son of Cuban immigrants who fled after Fidel Castro seized power in 1959, also raised the idea of regime change.
“If I lived in Havana, and I was in the government, I’d be concerned – at least a little bit,” Rubio said hours after Maduro’s abduction.
Rubio, who has long called for regime change in Cuba, is believed to be the main architect of plans to destabilise Havana by escalating the pressure on Venezuela.
Earlier this week, the US seized two sanctioned oil tankers as it sought to tighten its control over Venezuelan oil supplies.
“Blocking oil shipments has been a very big part because Cuba is suffering from severe fuel shortages,” Fulton Armstrong, a former US national intelligence officer for Latin America, said.
Citing three sources, ABC News reported on Wednesday that the Trump administration had told Venezuela’s interim president, Delcy Rodriguez, to meet US demands – including severing economic ties with China, Cuba, Iran and Russia – before being allowed to pump more oil.
Beijing is the largest buyer of Venezuela’s oil and has maintained strong ideological bonds with Cuba.
It has condemned the raid on Venezuela as a “clear violation of international law”, but observers believe its support will probably be limited.
“The shape of Cuba-China relations is for Beijing to determine. Cuba’s needs and desires are clear, but China has never been willing to be Cuba’s patron,” said Armstrong, now an adjunct professorial lecturer with American University in Washington.
Moscow had been Cuba’s main supporter since the 1960s, but the support ended abruptly when the Soviet Union collapsed in 1991.




Beijing has provided some funds and assistance and written off some of Cuba’s outstanding debt obligations.
But Armstrong said the relationship with Havana had never been a top priority for Beijing.
“While solidarity is important politically and psychologically, you can’t eat solidarity,” he said.
“Cuba needs concrete assistance for which China wants concrete payment, which Cuba doesn’t have. Maybe the current crisis will change China’s calculus, but my hunch is that the Chinese government will proceed with caution.”
When asked if China was willing to offer more financial or economic support to Venezuela and Cuba, foreign ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning said on Wednesday that it would continue to cooperate with these countries, but any specific measures would require a joint decision.
Jiang said China would avoid stepping into any conflict between Havana and Washington.
“This is in line with the core principle of Chinese foreign policy: to never provide military aid to one nation for the purpose of fighting another,” he said.
“Consequently, even though Cuba is in clear need of weaponry and military support, China has never offered such assistance.”
Cuba has been trying to strengthen ties with China, and the country has been one of its largest trading partner since the early 2000s, according to Adrian Hearn, a professor of Latin American studies at the University of Melbourne.
Chinese projects in Cuba range from energy, including renewables, to rice production and food processing.
In 2024, after direct flights were established, the number of Chinese tourists visiting rose by almost 50 per cent to around 27,000, according to official Cuban figures.
“Recent developments in Venezuela add further pressure to seek Chinese support, which may include more favourable investment terms,” Hearn said.
Armstrong said Beijing had long believed that Cuba needed to undertake reforms rather than expect handouts.
The US raid has prompted speculation that it has set a precedent that may serve Beijing’s interests in the future – for example, if it wanted to target the Taiwanese leadership.
“If that theory is correct, then it is reasonable to expect China to be cautious in making Cuba a point of confrontation with Washington,” he said.
Beijing views Taiwan as part of its territory and has never renounced the use of force to reunify it with the mainland. Most countries, including the US, do not recognise it as an independent state, but Washington opposes any attempt to take the island by force and is legally bound to provide arms to help it defend itself.
Jiang said it was unlikely that Latin American countries would yield to US pressure and China was not going to be deterred from continued engagement with the region.


A policy paper published weeks before the US raid said China’s relationships in Latin America and the Caribbean were “not directed at, do not exclude, and are not subject to the will of any third party”.
Jiang said: “The US will be desperate to exclude other countries, including China and Russia, outside the region. China’s bilateral ties with Latin American countries will definitely face more challenges in the future.”
The abduction of Maduro shocked Cuba and continuing US sanctions against Venezuela would “definitely hurt”, Armstrong said.
But he added: “Cuba has seen this coming for some time and has prepared.
“The preparations will not cover all needs – the pain will still be intense – but the ‘collapse’ of the Cuban government that Washington desires does not seem likely. The government has residual strengths.”

data.title: US raid in Venezuela lays bare its law-of-the-jungle approach
data.description:
US Secretary of Defence Pete Hegseth was in a belligerently high mood after the Pentagon’s successful operation to abduct Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro and his wife in the dead of night.
After all, the guy managed not to leak the US war plan in unsecure chat groups before the raid in Caracas, as he did with the US bombing of Houthi militias in Yemen in March. Remember that one?
Maybe I have a warped sense of humour, but I still find this passage from the chief editor of the Atlantic, Jeffrey Goldberg, among the funniest in the annals of US journalism. “The world found out shortly before 2pm eastern time on March 15 that the United States was bombing Houthi targets across Yemen,” he wrote. “I, however, knew two hours before the first bombs exploded that the attack might be coming. The reason I knew this is that Pete Hegseth, the secretary of defence, had texted me the war plan at 11.44am. The plan included precise information about weapons packages, targets and timing.”
Needless to say, defence officials are supposed to use secure channels to communicate. Such a failure would have been a career-ending moment for any US general or admiral, or rather, any US personnel in military uniform, if not worse.
But in the world of US President Donald Trump, normal professional standards and international norms don’t apply, and Hegseth suffered no serious consequences for a potentially fatal mistake.

The Trump administration has publicly praised the US press for withholding reports ahead of the Caracas raid, as several newspapers apparently knew about it beforehand. Did someone leak the plan, again?
It was, indeed, a very proud moment for Hegseth, a former co-host of a current affairs programme on Fox News. Speaking at Newport News Shipbuilding, the United States’ largest military shipyard, he said countries like Russia and China would “not dare” test the US following Maduro’s capture.
“We’re re-establishing deterrence that’s so absolute and so unquestioned that our enemies will not dare to test us,” Hegseth said. “They’re noticing American power. They’re noticing American strength. They’re noticing American clarity and leadership.”
Actually, while they publicly condemned the US operation, the Russians and Chinese probably have a very different take. The attack on Venezuela, as a topic, garnered hundreds of millions of views on Weibo. Many draw the obvious conclusion that it could be used as a military template against the island of Taiwan.
Chinese analysts interviewed by the Post agreed. Among them was mainland aviation analyst Fu Qianshao, who implied separatist leaders in Taiwan could face a similar fate. “The US operation to capture Maduro should become a clear warning to Taiwan’s leadership,” he said. “Taiwan must consider whether it could evade a similar action if the PLA were to carry it out.”
It’s not just mainland Chinese. Taiwanese leaders may have put on a brave face, but some experts on the island are warning that something like Maduro’s capture could happen to them.

According to Su Tzu-yun of the Taiwanese government-funded Institute for National Defence and Security Research, Beijing had long sought to map Taiwan’s leadership movements and command vulnerabilities through infiltration and intelligence collection.
Chen Kuo-ming, editor of the Taipei-based Defence International magazine, warned Beijing could learn from the US “decapitation-style” operation by exploiting “any window created by a shift in US focus towards South America to intensify military pressure or harassment against Taiwan”.
Meanwhile, Hegseth has openly mocked the ineffectiveness of Russia’s air defences deployed by Venezuela.
Trump said the United States would “run” Venezuela, and US oil companies would take over its primary natural resource, which is the largest oil reserves in the world. He has also ruled out holding elections for now and refused to support opposition leader Maria Corina Machado, last year’s Nobel Peace Prize winner who has long supported US military intervention.


According to numerous reports and the Ukrainian government, Moscow has repeatedly tried to kill or capture the country’s president, Volodymyr Zelensky, since 2022.
What Washington has done shows Americans are just better at kidnapping heads of state than Russians. Trump has repeatedly warned of military actions in Mexico and Colombia, and doubled down on taking over Greenland from Denmark.
But what about that rules-based international order? It’s now more like the law of the jungle. You can be pretty sure that the Russians are reaching similar conclusions as the Chinese.
Seeing what Trump wants to do in the western hemisphere, might not Russian President Vladimir Putin claim the same carte blanche across the former Soviet Union?

data.title: China cautions against ‘compound errors’ from clashing policies
data.description:
A mouthpiece for China’s ruling Communist Party has warned against conflicting economic measures, urging officials to better guide market expectations in a front-page commentary highlighting Beijing’s growing focus on policy coordination.
“The more policies there are, the greater the risk they will be working at odds,” the People’s Daily editorial said on Friday, pointing to China’s increasingly complex economic environment, “with more and more policy dilemmas emerging and greater demands on policy”.
It also cautioned against “compound errors” – where inconsistent policies cancel each other out – noting that such mistakes have previously “unsettled market expectations”. Describing the market economy as largely an “expectations economy”, the piece called for stronger policy narratives and prompt responses to market concerns.
The market has expressed concerns in recent years over different policies from various government departments, which have at times clouded business and investor expectations. Periods of regulatory tightening have sometimes been followed by easing or recalibration, making it difficult for market participants to gauge policy direction.
Friday’s commentary was the third in a series released this week, coming at the start of China’s 15th five-year planning period and published under the pen name “Zhong Caiping”. The pseudonym resembles one – “Zhong Caiwen” – that is widely believed to represent the Central Financial and Economic Affairs Commission, a key economic decision-making body headed by President Xi Jinping.
The previous two instalments, published since Wednesday, respectively warned against industrial “involution” – referring to cutthroat, low-quality competition – and highlighted China’s economic resilience.
Efforts should be coordinated to maximise the combined effect of existing and incremental policies
People’s Daily editorial
Policymakers will introduce incremental measures in 2026 based on changing circumstances, according to Friday’s editorial.
“Efforts should be coordinated to maximise the combined effect of existing and incremental policies, to support steady and positive economic growth,” it added, echoing similar calls from December’s tone-setting central economic work conference.
On the currency front, the mouthpiece stressed the need to uphold the market’s role in setting exchange rates while keeping the yuan basically stable at a reasonable and balanced level.
For the first time since 2024, the offshore yuan recently strengthened past the threshold of 7.0 per US dollar and has remained resilient despite the American currency’s broad strength following US military action in Venezuela that resulted in the abduction of its leader, Nicolas Maduro. A lower figure reflects a stronger yuan, as it takes fewer yuan to equate to a dollar.
Morgan Stanley has raised its yuan forecast to 6.85 per dollar in the first quarter of this year, up from 7.05, citing strong exports as support while deflationary pressure remains a drag, according to a research note by the investment bank on Thursday.
Analysts at Goldman Sachs said in a note on Monday that while policymakers may signal their preference for a stronger yuan, recent policy communications suggest “the central bank is keen to avoid too fast an appreciation”.

data.title: China says it is still ‘deeply committed’ to Venezuela as ambassador meets new leader
data.description:
China said on Friday it would continue to offer its firm support to Venezuela after its ambassador met the country’s interim president Delcy Rodriguez.
She described her meeting with Chinese ambassador Lan Hu as “cordial” and thanked Beijing for its condemnation of the abduction of former leader Nicolas Maduro and his wife Cilia Flores.
“We value China’s firm and consistent stance in strongly condemning the serious violation of international law and Venezuelan sovereignty,” Rodriguez said.
Responding to a question about the meeting, Chinese foreign ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning said China valued its relations with Venezuela and had always maintained good communication and cooperation with the Venezuelan government.
“China will continue to firmly support Venezuela in safeguarding sovereignty, dignity and national security,” Mao said.
“No matter how the political situation may evolve in Venezuela, China is deeply committed to deepening practical cooperation across many fields and promoting common development.”
Venezuela’s Supreme Court appointed Rodríguez, the former vice-president, as interim president on Monday. The ambassadors of China, Russia and Iran were among the diplomats present at the ceremony.
China’s investments and wider relationship with Venezuela have been plunged into uncertainty following last weekend’s raid and Donald Trump’s claim that Venezuela would transfer between 30 and 50 million barrels of oil to the US.


Venezuela, which holds the world’s largest known crude oil reserves, has seen its oil production decline sharply over the past decade due to sanctions and the exodus of foreign companies.
Restoring its oil industry will require billions of dollars in investment, making it harder for the US to use its resources to exert pressure.
Washington has been pressuring Venezuela to cut economic ties with China, Russia, Iran and Cuba. This week it also seized two sanctioned oil tankers, a move many analysts saw as a sign it planned to continue its partial blockade to keep up the pressure on the Venezuelan government.
But on Tuesday, Rodriguez insisted: “The government of Venezuela is in charge in our country, and no one else.”
China remains a key economic and diplomatic ally of Venezuela and is the largest importer of Venezuelan crude oil.
The country is also a key player in Beijing’s Belt and Road Initiative, offering access to minerals, infrastructure projects and markets.
Beijing has consistently opposed US sanctions and maintains strong ties to Venezuela’s oil sector through joint ventures such as PetroSinovensa, operated by China National Petroleum Corporation and Venezuela’s PDVSA.
Some analysts have warned that a pro-US government could review or even revoke existing contracts, possibly diluting Chinese stakes or even replacing them with American firms.
Some sectors, particularly telecommunications, could also face stricter scrutiny or bans on Chinese technology as a result of national security concerns.
This week Mao has repeatedly emphasised that China’s cooperation with Venezuela was rooted in mutual respect between “two sovereign states.”




She stressed that China’s rights and interests in Venezuela must be protected, and condemned what she called “blatant interference” and a “typical act of bullying” by the US.
Venezuelan Foreign Minister Yvan Gil has thanked China for its “support and solidarity in the face of the recent US military aggression”.
“Venezuela reaffirms its commitment to deepening trade and economic agreements with the People’s Republic of China,” he wrote on social media.
He added that relations between the two countries were “supported by the framework of international law and the regulations of both sovereign nations”.

data.title: Malaysia’s Umno seeks Najib’s pardon amid 1MDB scandal, while staying in PM Anwar’s coalition
data.description:
The United Malays National Organisation (Umno), which for decades dominated Malaysian politics and remains a key member of the ruling coalition, is set to renew a push for the release of jailed former prime minister Najib Razak next week, even as it steps back from threats to quit the government.
Resolutions pushing for a full pardon and demanding justice for Najib were the “hottest topic” submitted by the party’s 191 divisions ahead of its annual assembly, Secretary General Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki said at a briefing on Friday. Some 104 divisions submitted resolutions calling for the release of Najib, who has multiple convictions for his role in the 1MDB fraud that cost the country billions.
The resolutions show the strength of support retained by Najib, 72, who last month lost a bid to serve the remainder of a six-year prison sentence at home. He was subsequently convicted of abuse of power and money laundering, and handed a US$2.8 billion fine and a further 15 years in prison. All the convictions were in connection with 1MDB, a failed state-run fund whose collapse sparked probes from Singapore to Switzerland.


Asyraf said that despite recent calls for the party to leave Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s government – triggered by another coalition member’s gloating over Najib’s failed bid for house arrest – no resolution to withdraw has been submitted to the party ahead of the meeting.
“It’s not going to be a big issue in this year’s assembly,” Asyraf said.
Umno Youth chief Akmal Saleh last week called for the party to leave the government. The youth wing would propose that Umno revive its cooperation with the opposition Pan-Malaysian Islamic Party (PAS).
Umno president Ahmad Zahid Hamidi has previously shot down suggestions that the party should leave the government, reiterating a commitment to remain in the coalition until the next nationwide election that must be held by early 2028. Ahmad Zahid serves as deputy prime minister in Anwar’s cabinet, and saw his own corruption case dropped by prosecutors days before the assembly, which runs from January 14 through January 17.

Any bid for a pardon must go through Malaysia’s current king, Sultan Ibrahim Iskandar. Najib previously had a sentence reduced by Ibrahim’s predecessor, Sultan Abdullah Ahmad, who hails from the former premier’s home state of Pahang.
Najib was ousted when Umno, which had ruled Malaysia for six decades, lost power in 2018 after the 1MDB scandal. It became part of Anwar’s coalition in 2022.

data.title: 8 arrested over attempting to defraud HK$8.24 million from Hong Kong subsidy scheme
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested eight people for allegedly attempting to defraud HK$8.24 million (US$1.06 million) from a subsidy scheme meant to help companies boost employees’ technology skills, with a family suspected of running the swindle.
Chen Chi-cheong, assistant district commander (crime) of Sham Shui Po police district, said on Friday that officers had busted a suspected fraud syndicate this week.
The eight suspects were believed to have targeted the government’s “New Industrialisation and Technology Training Programme” between 2022 and 2024.
“The syndicate established companies that had no substantial operations, showing an organised and systematic attempt to defraud public funds intended for promoting innovation,” Chen said.
The suspects consisted of six men and two women, aged 33 to 78. They include five members of the same family, comprising a couple, their two sons and a partner of one son.
The force said the family were suspected of being the scheme’s masterminds.
Chief Inspector Cheng Ki-fung said the Vocational Training Council (VTC), which was the programme’s secretariat, had referred the case to the police at the end of 2024 after an ad hoc check of some participants had raised suspicions.
Under the scheme’s rules, training providers and companies could apply for subsidies if they enrolled full-time staff to take part in technology-related courses. But participating companies could not be associated with programme organisers.
During the check, three participants told the VTC that they were hired as part-time secret reviewers to evaluate the programme and had not realised they were supposed to be representing an employer when attending the training as full-time staff.
Cheng said the investigation suggested the family had set up a company to provide training courses for “employees” of 11 other businesses they had founded.
They then applied for subsidies through both the course provider and the other companies, which recruited “staff members” via social media to attend the courses, he said.
The suspects also collected the personal details of the people they hired and told the secretariat that relevant employees were all full-time staff at their network of companies, registering them for courses operated by the syndicate’s programme provider.
The chief inspector said officers had discovered that the companies involved in this case had submitted false documents to the secretariat, including fake employment and training attendance records.
“Our investigations have discovered there were 12 companies suspected of conspiracy to defraud the relevant training subsidies,” Cheng said.
“The relationships between these companies have seriously violated the conflict of interest declaration under the scheme.”
Police said the 12 businesses allegedly linked to the suspects had applied for subsidies worth HK$8.24 million between August 2022 and December 2024.
The companies only received HK$640,000 of the funds, with the force preventing the rest of the money from being disbursed as part of its investigations.
Senior Inspector Wan Tin-sing said officers had confiscated 11 computers suspected to contain relevant documents, as well as company stamps, chequebooks and other documents while making arrests this week.
Wan said the eight arrestees claimed to be businesspeople, homemakers or unemployed.
In Hong Kong, the maximum penalty for conspiracy to defraud is 14 years in prison.
Alexander Leung Siu-kwun, senior manager of the Innovation and Technology Commission’s innovation and technology fund, said the scheme was rolled out in August 2018 to subsidise local enterprises that enrolled their staff in high-end technology training related to emerging industries.
He said each participating company could have received up to HK$250,000 worth of subsidies per financial year, with each employee permitted to be subsidised for one course for the same period.
“The secretariat has set up a stringent evaluation mechanism, including ad hoc spot checks,” Leung said.

data.title: Are the Communist Party and the KMT reviving a cross-strait political channel?
data.description:
The Communist Party and Taiwan’s Kuomintang (KMT) appear to be in the final stages of talks to revive an inter-party forum suspended nine years ago.
The three-day forum, designed to facilitate party-to-party communication and promote cross-strait exchanges, was scheduled to start in Beijing on January 27, Taiwan’s China Times newspaper reported on Friday.
If it takes place, it will be the first time the event has been held since 2016, when it was suspended because of tensions between the mainland and Taiwan and debate within the KMT. It was held 11 times before then.
The development represents a renewed effort by Beijing and Taiwan’s main opposition party to restore inter-party channels at a critical moment.
Cross-strait relations began declining in 2016 when Tsai Ing-wen became the island’s leader and have deteriorated under her successor, William Lai Ching-te.
Late last year, the People’s Liberation Army staged a round of massive military drills near Taiwan, saying they were aimed at deterring “Taiwan independence forces” and external intervention.
The forum is also expected to pave the way for a meeting between newly elected KMT chairwoman Cheng Li-wun and Chinese President Xi Jinping, which some media outlets have suggested could occur as soon as March.
Cheng has repeatedly expressed hopes for a face-to-face meeting with Xi and supported the idea of a strong Chinese identity and reconciliation with the mainland. She said on Friday that she would give more details about the forum on Saturday.
Cheng’s deputy, KMT vice-chairman Hsiao Hsu-tsen, is expected to head the Taiwanese delegation at the forum, according to Taiwan media reports.
Beijing’s Taiwan Affairs Office did not respond to a request for comment.


The forum was first held in 2006 and called the Cross-Strait Economic and Trade Forum.
It followed the historic 2005 meeting in Beijing between then-Communist Party general secretary Hu Jintao and then-KMT chairman Lien Chan, which resulted in a five-point consensus, including the establishment of a party-to-party communication platform.
Over the following decade, proposals from the event covered a wide range of issues, including agriculture, industrial and financial cooperation, tourism and people-to-people exchanges.
The forum was renamed the Cross-Strait Peaceful Development Forum in November 2016, just months after the Democratic Progressive Party took power in Taiwan. Then-KMT leader Hung Hsiu-chu took part in the gathering and met Xi before the forum as part of her trip to the mainland.
Since then, there has been a lack of communication channels between the KMT and the Communist Party. There have also been no official channels between the two sides of the strait since the DPP came to power.
Zhang Wensheng, deputy dean and a professor at the Taiwan Research Institute at Xiamen University, said the forum would help to ease cross-strait tensions as well as promote cross-strait exchanges.
But given the KMT was not in power, “it would have little ability to resolve the fundamental political differences across the strait”, Zhang said.
According to mainland cross-strait analyst Zhu Songling, the forum would be Cheng’s first effort to navigate cross-strait relations and consolidate her authority within the party.
But Zhu warned that there would be few breakthroughs.
“Given the timing and cross-strait political atmosphere, if the forum does go ahead, the political symbolism will far outweigh substantive administrative breakthroughs,” said Zhu, a professor at the Institute of Taiwan Studies at Beijing Union University.


Beijing has repeatedly criticised Lai’s pro-independence stance and launched four large-scale military exercises since he took office in May 2024. The latest, a two-day drill dubbed “Justice Mission-2025”, was held at the end of December.
On Friday, the DPP raised doubts about the event, asking what the KMT might be offering in exchange for attending the forum.
Citing the KMT’s decision to block Taiwan’s defence budget in the Legislative Yuan, the DPP said: “Taiwan’s security should not be used as leverage to curry favour with the Chinese Communist Party.”
Zhu also noted that, because the KMT was not the ruling party, any outcomes from the forum – such as resuming tourism or flights – could become “empty promises” if the DPP refused to implement them.
KMT spokesman Niu Hsu-ting said on Friday that if the forum could be reconvened, it would mean there was still a channel for dialogue between the two parties.
“The outside world should view it with a more open mind, especially given the rapidly changing international situation and rising geopolitical risks. Communication itself is necessary,” he said.

data.title: Hong Kong sets up 13-member expert panel to help mainland firms expand overseas
data.description:
Hong Kong’s Department of Justice has set up a 13-member expert committee to advise on strategies for promoting the city’s legal and professional services sector to mainland Chinese businesses seeking to expand overseas.
The department on Friday said the new Expert Committee on Professional Services for Going Global, chaired by Deputy Secretary for Justice Horace Cheung Kwok-kwan, would build upon the GoGlobal platform authorities launched in December.
Cheung said the committee would gather “strategic advice from members of different professional services sectors” to help mainland enterprises.
The justice department said the 13 members would serve a two-year term and comprise experts from the legal, financial and accounting sectors from the mainland and Hong Kong, as well as business and corporate representatives.
Among the members for the first two-year term were Legislative Councillor Maggie Chan Man-ki and Hong Kong Trade Development Council deputy executive director Patrick Lau Hui-ping.
The department said the committee would provide advice and help on enhancing the understanding of the needs of mainland enterprises expanding overseas.

It would also coordinate with Hong Kong’s professional services sectors, including the legal, accounting and financial sectors, to support mainland enterprises going global.
The establishment of the expert committee follows the launch of the Hong Kong Professional Services GoGlobal Platform last month.
The platform aims to coordinate the city’s professional services to meet the needs of mainland enterprises and will collaborate with the Hong Kong Trade Development Council in making the support deeper and broader.
It will integrate information related to the city’s professional sectors and publish lists of service providers supporting overseas expansion.
At the time, Cheung said the platform would also complement the Task Force on Supporting Mainland Enterprises in Going Global that launched in October.
The task force is one of the efforts outlined in Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu’s policy address in September to help mainland enterprises build their operations overseas.

data.title: Chinese firms outpace US rivals in 2025 humanoid robot shipments, as AgiBot takes lead
data.description:
Shanghai-based AgiBot topped global humanoid robot shipments in 2025, with nearly a 38 per cent share, as China’s robotics firms dominated the market, leaving in the dust major US players like Elon Musk’s Tesla.
According to data released on Thursday by Omdia, AgiBot shipped 5,168 humanoid robots last year to lead five other Chinese companies in the research firm’s top 10 list.
Unitree Robotics, headquartered in Hangzhou, ranked second with 4,200 humanoids shipped last year, which accounted for 32 per cent of the market.
Hong Kong-listed UBTech Robotics, based in Shenzhen, was ranked third with 1,000 units shipped.
Leju Robotics, Engine AI and Fourier Intelligence ranked fourth, fifth and sixth in 2025 shipments, according to Omdia.
Shipments worldwide jumped nearly 480 per cent in 2025 to 13,318 units, according to Omdia. It projected the volume to reach 2.6 million units in 2035.
“Chinese vendors are setting benchmarks in large-scale production, as they have reached thousand-unit shipments in a short period, enabling the deployment of tens of thousands of robots annually,” Omdia analyst Lian Jye Su said in the report.

The 2025 shipment figures reflected how far Chinese manufacturers have pulled ahead in this fast-developing sector. These firms had issued five times as many related patents as the US over the past five years, Morgan Stanley said in its latest Robot Almanac.
By comparison, US robot maker Tesla shipped only 150 units in 2025, which accounted for 1 per cent of the global market, according to Omdia. American firms Figure AI and Agility Robotics also shipped 150 units each last year.
The scaled-up production by Chinese manufacturers was driven by a combination of favourable policies, infrastructure support and investments in the public and private sectors, Omdia’s Su said.
Embodied intelligence – a branch of AI that operates through a physical body – was identified by the Chinese government as a key future industry for the first time in a work report delivered at the annual meeting of the National People’s Congress last March.


With strong government support, China has witnessed a boom in the humanoid robot sector in recent years. The country’s economic planning agency, the National Development and Reform Commission, even flagged the risks of excessive duplication in the sector in November.
Major Chinese robotics firms have ramped up their commercialisation efforts amid growing competition. They have formed partnerships with the broader manufacturing industry to enable large-scale deployment of their robots on factory floors.
In August, AgiBot secured an order from car parts manufacturer Fulin Precision to deploy nearly 100 robots across its factories. In July, AgiBot and Unitree landed orders totalling 124 million yuan (US$17.7 million) from China Mobile.

data.title: Hong Kong just had its driest January day. But how does that impact your health?
data.description:
Hong Kong experienced its driest January day on record earlier this week, bucking its reputation for high temperatures and humidity.
Relative humidity in the city plunged to 17 per cent on Thursday, the lowest for the month since records started in 1984.
To help Hongkongers adjust, the Post speaks to experts about how to manage the cold and dry weather.
1. What is the impact on the respiratory system?
Dr Edmund Lam Wing-wo, a family doctor, said the sudden cold and dry weather was conducive to the proliferation of respiratory viruses and could weaken people’s immune systems, including their nasal mucosa response.
“If viruses become more rampant and people’s immune systems weaken, it becomes easier to catch infections ranging from colds to flu,” he said on Friday.
He noted that other infections, such as those caused by respiratory syncytial virus or rhinovirus, could also be more prevalent because of the dry and cold weather, despite being present year round.
Lam said wearing surgical masks outdoors could help, as the air people breathed in could be less dry and cold. He also reminded the public to put on enough clothing and drink enough water to stay hydrated.
Lam said those with asthma could keep their condition under control with the help of inhalers, but they should seek medical advice if their symptoms worsened.
He added that the weather could also worsen existing cases of respiratory illnesses, such as colds and coughs.

2. What about moisturising?
Lam said his family clinic had, by Friday, seen several patients whose skin was so itchy that they could not sleep or were bleeding after scratching themselves.
Such symptoms were particularly common among the elderly or those suffering from atopic eczema.
He reminded residents to moisturise their skin with creams and avoid using scorching hot water for showers despite the cold weather.
Those seeking medical advice could ask for ointments with steroids, which would not have any side effects if used for a short period, he said.
“But if you have not had medical advice and the itching is severe, you could visit a doctor. You may have wounded the skin through scratching and developed an infection, which may require treatment with antibiotics,” Lam said.
3. How can my diet help me stay hydrated?
Bonnie Leung Wing-yan, a dietitian from the Hong Kong Community Dietitian Association, stressed the importance of drinking water in a dry environment and suggested that daily consumption should be 30 to 35ml per kilogram of body weight.
She added that consuming omega-3 fatty acids could help maintain cell membrane integrity, reduce the loss of moisture, improve skin barrier function and hydration capacity. They can be found in salmon, yellow croaker, mackerel, flaxseed, chia seeds, walnuts and avocados.
Foods such as carrots and pumpkins, which are rich in vitamin A, could help maintain healthy skin epithelial cells and promote skin repair, while vitamin C-rich foods such as kiwi fruits and strawberries were good for collagen synthesis, she said.
The dietitian said nuts and vegetable oils, which contain a lot of vitamin E, could protect cell membranes and reduce skin moisture loss.

She added that different types of tea, including floral and fruit varieties, were also good for hydration.
Chinese-style soups rich in antioxidants and vitamins A and E were also good for dry and cold weather, including one cooked with lily bulbs, cashews, pumpkin and snow fungus, or those made with black sesame, black beans, walnuts and figs.
“When the weather is cold, the body might want to eat more to stay warm and could unintentionally consume too much,” she said.
“I suggest maintaining a balanced diet with primarily vegetables and proteins and less fat to avoid excess calories that could lead to extra pounds.”
